root_text.sn46.21:1.1: Atha kho aññataro bhikkhu yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami …pe… ekamantaṁ nisinno kho so bhikkhu bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn46.21:2.3: “‘Bodhāya saṁvattantī’ti kho, bhikkhu, tasmā bojjhaṅgāti vuccanti.
root_text.sn46.21:2.5: upekkhāsambojjhaṅgaṁ bhāveti vivekanissitaṁ virāganissitaṁ nirodhanissitaṁ vossaggapariṇāmiṁ.

translation_text.sn46.21:1.1: Then a mendicant went up to the Buddha … and said to him:
translation_text.sn46.21:2.2: How are the awakening factors defined?”
translation_text.sn46.21:2.3: “Mendicant, they’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening.
translation_text.sn46.21:2.4: A mendicant develops the awakening factors of mindfulness, investigation of principles, energy, rapture, tranquility, immersion,
translation_text.sn46.21:2.5: and equanimity, which rely on seclusion, fading away, and cessation, and ripen as letting go.
translation_text.sn46.21:2.6: They’re called awakening factors because they lead to awakening.”
